title: Chord Length Calculator - Find Chord, Radius, or Central Angle
tagline: Calculate chord length from radius and central angle, find radius from chord and angle, or determine the central angle from radius and chord. Free online tool for circle geometry.
intro: Select what you want to calculate, choose degree or radian input, enter the two known values, and get an instant result with the formula.
formula: Formula: chord = 2r × sin(θ/2), where r is the radius and θ is the central angle

type.findChord_desc: Enter the radius and central angle to compute the straight-line distance between the two endpoints of the chord on the circle.
type.findRadius_desc: Enter the chord length and central angle to reverse-engineer the radius of the circle using r = c / (2 × sin(θ/2)).
type.findAngle_desc: Enter the radius and chord length to determine the central angle subtended by the chord using θ = 2 × arcsin(c / (2r)).

error.invalid: Please enter valid numbers in all required fields.
error.positiveRadius: Radius must be a positive number.
error.positiveChord: Chord length must be a positive number.
error.chordTooLong: Chord length cannot exceed the diameter (2 × radius).
error.zeroAngle: Central angle cannot be zero or a multiple of 2π.

examples.heading: Chord length calculator examples
examples.intro: Three worked examples illustrating each of the three calculation modes with realistic values.

items.note: chord = 2 × 10 × sin(30°) = 20 × 0.5 = 10. A 60° central angle in a circle of radius 10 produces a chord equal to the radius.
r = 10 / (2 × sin(45°)) = 10 / (2 × 0.7071) ≈ 7.07. Useful when you measure a chord in the field and know the arc spans a right angle.
θ = 2 × arcsin(5/10) = 2 × 30° = 60°. An equilateral triangle inscribed in a circle has all chords equal to the radius and all central angles equal to 60°.

howto.heading: How to use the chord length calculator
steps: Choose the calculation mode: 'Find Chord Length' if you know the radius and central angle, 'Find Radius' if you know chord length and angle, or 'Find Central Angle' if you know radius and chord.
Select whether your angle is in degrees or radians using the angle unit buttons.
Enter the two known values in the input fields that appear for the selected mode.
Click 'Calculate' to see the result with the formula used for the computation.
Use the example buttons to load pre-set values and explore how chord, radius, and angle relate to each other in different scenarios.

faq.heading: Chord length calculator FAQ
faq.q1: What is a chord in geometry?
faq.q1_a: A chord is a straight line segment connecting any two points on the circumference of a circle. The diameter is the longest possible chord, passing through the center and subtending a central angle of 180°. All other chords are shorter than the diameter and subtend smaller central angles.
faq.q2: What is the chord length formula?
faq.q2_a: The standard formula is chord = 2r × sin(θ/2), where r is the circle's radius and θ is the central angle in radians or degrees. It derives from the law of cosines applied to the isosceles triangle formed by two radii and the chord. You can rearrange it to find radius as r = c / (2 × sin(θ/2)) or angle as θ = 2 × arcsin(c / (2r)).
faq.q3: Can a chord be longer than the diameter?
faq.q3_a: No. The diameter is the maximum chord length for any circle. If you enter a chord length greater than twice the radius, the calculator will report an error because no valid central angle exists for that combination. Mathematically, the argument of arcsin would exceed 1, which has no real solution.
faq.q4: How do I convert between degrees and radians?
faq.q4_a: Multiply degrees by π/180 to get radians, or multiply radians by 180/π to get degrees. For example, 60° equals π/3 ≈ 1.0472 radians, and π/2 ≈ 1.5708 radians equals 90°. This calculator accepts both units and converts internally, so you can work in whichever system you prefer.
faq.q5: What is the difference between chord length and arc length?
faq.q5_a: Chord length is the straight-line distance between two points on the circle. Arc length is the distance along the curved circumference between the same two points. Arc length equals r × θ (with θ in radians), while chord length equals 2r × sin(θ/2). For small angles the two are nearly equal, but they diverge significantly for large central angles.
faq.q6: Where are chord length calculations used in practice?
faq.q6_a: Chord calculations appear in architecture (arches, curved beams), mechanical engineering (gear tooth geometry, cam profiles), surveying (computing horizontal distances from arc measurements), and structural analysis (bridge arch design). Woodworking and metalworking tradespeople use the formula when cutting curved stock to fit a specific span.

about.heading: About the chord length calculator
about.body:
A chord is one of the fundamental line segments in circle geometry. It connects any two distinct points on a circle's circumference and divides the interior of the circle into two regions. The diameter is the special case of a chord that passes through the center, and it is the longest chord in any circle. Every other chord is strictly shorter than the diameter and subtends a central angle strictly less than 180°.

The relationship between chord length, radius, and central angle is captured by the formula c = 2r × sin(θ/2), where c is the chord length, r is the radius, and θ is the central angle in radians. This formula derives from the law of cosines. If you draw radii to both endpoints of the chord, you create an isosceles triangle with two sides of length r and the included angle θ. Applying the law of cosines gives c² = 2r²(1 − cos θ). Using the half-angle identity 1 − cos θ = 2 sin²(θ/2), this simplifies to c = 2r sin(θ/2).

The calculator supports three modes corresponding to the three ways you can rearrange this formula. In 'Find Chord Length' mode, you supply r and θ and the calculator evaluates 2r sin(θ/2) directly. In 'Find Radius' mode, you supply c and θ and the calculator evaluates r = c / (2 sin(θ/2)), which is useful when you have physically measured a chord and know the arc spans a specific angle. In 'Find Central Angle' mode, you supply r and c and the calculator evaluates θ = 2 arcsin(c / (2r)), which yields the angle whose sine is c/(2r).

Angle units can be freely switched between degrees and radians. Degrees are more intuitive for most users and are the default, but engineering and scientific work often uses radians because arc length = r × θ only holds in radians. The calculator converts internally so you can use whichever system your source data uses.

Practical applications of chord calculations are numerous. In structural engineering, arch bridges are designed using the relationship between span (chord), rise, and radius of curvature. In mechanical engineering, involute gear tooth geometry depends on chord calculations across pitch circles. Surveyors use chord length combined with arc measurements to compute distances when curves interrupt line-of-sight measurements. In woodworking, a craftsperson who needs to cut a curved piece of wood to span a certain distance at a certain angle uses exactly this formula. Navigation historically used chord tables — precursors to modern trigonometric tables — to compute distances on spherical surfaces.

This calculator handles any valid input combination within the physical constraints: radii must be positive, chord lengths must be positive and at most twice the radius, and angles must be positive and less than 360° (or 2π radians). Results are rounded to eight decimal places, which is more than sufficient precision for any practical application.
